data.title: 10 Strong Buy listed stocks that are in the green YTD as Wall Street sinks
data.description:
As Wall Street's major market averages continue to experience declines in the aftermath of the new tariffs imposed by President Trump on April 2, which have impacted nations worldwide, not all companies have seen the same level of suffering, with some names faring better than others in these challenging times.

Outlined below are ten U.S. based stocks with market caps of at least $10B that trade both in positive territory in 2025 and hold Strong Buy ratings according to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Metrics. List is ranked per SA quant grades.

No. 10: Ameren Corporation (NYSE:AEE), quant rating of 4.54. 

No. 9: AngloGold Ashanti plc (NYSE:AU), quant rating of 4.57.

No. 8: W. P. Carey (NYSE:WPC), quant rating of 4.63. 

No. 7: Take-Two Interactive Software (NASDAQ:TTWO), quant rating of 4.63. 

No. 6: Uber Technologies (NYSE:UBER), quant rating of 4.77.

No. 5: RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX), quant rating of 4.77. 

No. 4: Philip Morris International (NYSE:PM), quant rating of 4.78.

No. 3: Pilgrim's Pride Corporation (NASDAQ:PPC), quant rating of 4.90. 

No. 2: Exelon Corporation (NASDAQ:EXC), quant rating of 4.95. 

No. 1: Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B), quant rating of 4.95. 

For reference, Seeking Alpha's Quant system is driven by powerful computer processing and SA's special 'Quantamental' analysis. It awards grades to stocks based on collective value, growth, profitability, earnings per share revisions, and price momentum metrics. 

Year-to-date performance: AEE +5.7%, AU +43.6%, WPC +3%, TTWO +6.6%, UBER +7.8%, RTX +4.1%, PM +24%, PPC +8.1%, EXC +18.2%, and BRK.B +8.6%.

data.title: Trump is said to prepare executive order to revive U.S. shipbuilding
data.description:
President Trump is preparing to sign an executive order as early as Wednesday intended to revive U.S. shipbuilding and to loosen China’s grip on the global shipping industry, a Reuters reporter said in a social-media post that cited sources familiar with the matter.

Huntington Ingalls Industries' (NYSE:HII) stock was up 2.2% by 12:20 p.m. ET amid a broader advance among other aerospace and defense companies. General Dynamics (NYSE:GD) rose 2.7%.

data.title: RxSight downgraded at UBS after Q1 miss and guidance cut
data.description:
RxSight (NASDAQ:RXST) continued to trade lower on Wednesday after UBS downgraded the maker of intraocular lenses following the company’s recent decision to slash its full-year outlook on the back of lower-than-expected preliminary Q1 revenue.

Shares of the Aliso Viejo, California-based firm sold off last week after the announcement, which prompted Bank of America to immediately downgrade the company to Underperform from Buy.

On Tuesday, UBS analyst Danielle Antalffy lowered her rating to Neutral from Buy after revising her two-year CAGR estimates for the company to 15% from 21% CAGR previously and citing a longer path to profitability.

“While we believe long-term growth momentum may not be a significant concern, the broader market faces persistent headwinds, exacerbated by recent tariff policies that heighten recession fears,” Antalffy wrote as she slashed her price target to $16 from $45 per share.

In terms of EV/revenue, the analyst argued that RXST should trade around 3x its 2026 sales, as the company’s SMID-cap peers with similar growth profiles and longer paths to profitability trade at low single-digit multiples.

data.title: Memory stocks in focus as Google says AI hypercomputer runs on new TPU
data.description:
• Memory stocks were in focus on Wednesday as Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (GOOGL) said its new artificial intelligence hypercomputer will run on its latest memory-heavy tensor processing unit.
  • Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) shares fell 1% in midday trading, while American Depository Receipts of Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and SK Hnix (OTCPK:HXSCF) were little changed.
  • “Our new seventh-generation TPU, Ironwood, is designed specifically for thinking and inferential AI models,” Google said in a blog post, referencing its latest TPU. “Ironwood offers five times more peak compute capacity and six times the high-bandwidth memory (HBM) capacity compared to the prior-generation TPU.”
  • Micron, Samsung and SK Hnix largely control the memory market, according to research firm TechInsights.

data.title: Airbus delivers 71 planes in March, lifting year's total to 136
data.description:
Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF) (OTCPK:EADSY) on Wednesday said it had delivered 71 commercial airplanes to customers in March, lifting its year-to-date total to 136.

The European aviation giant's tally comes a day after U.S. rival Boeing (NYSE:BA) said it had delivered 41 planes in March, raising its total to 130 for the year.

data.title: TikTok U.S. deal must comply with Chinese law - Beijing spokesperson
data.description:
Any deal for TikTok U.S. (BDNCE) has to be in compliance with the Chinese law, a spokesperson for China’s commerce ministry said Wednesday.

The statement from China comes following a deadline extension by U.S. President Donald Trump for the short video content app from the sale-or-ban law passed under the Biden administration.

The spokesperson for the country said China "opposes practices that ignore the laws of the market economy, plunder by force, and damage the legitimate rights and interests of enterprises. The specific business arrangements must comply with Chinese law, including the export of technology, which must be approved by the Chinese government."

A deal to spin off TikTok's U.S. operations was put on hold after the Chinese government indicated it would not approve the deal under Trump's reciprocal tariff threat.

Earlier this month, a spokesperson for ByteDance told Seeking Alpha that the company "has been in discussion with the U.S. government regarding a potential solution for TikTok U.S. An agreement has not been executed. There are key matters to be resolved. Any agreement will be subject to approval under Chinese law."

data.title: Apollo CEO: The Fed could step in to stabilize markets if turmoil lasts
data.description:
Marc Rowan, Apollo Global Management CEO, said he sees the Federal Reserve stepping in to stabilize the economy if the capital markets become fundamentally dislocated.

“During Covid, the Fed was buying $100B of Treasury bonds a day,” Rowan said in a CNBC interview. The Fed's purchase of securities was aimed at improving the functioning of the Treasury and MBS markets.

In addition, he said he expects President Donald Trump’s administration to “resolve” the financially oriented tariff deals over the near-term.

He believes the Trump administration will work on deals with most countries over the next 90 days.

However, “those deals that involve non-tariff barriers, particularly with the EU, will be much harder to make and will take longer,” he said.

data.title: Capri gains on report Prada close to Versace deal after tariff concern discount
data.description:
Capri Holdings (NYSE:CPRI) rose 5% amid reports that Prada (OTCPK:PRDSF) is close to acquiring the Versace brand for about $1.38 billion.

Prada (OTCPK:PRDSF) is getting a $200 million discount from an earlier expected price tag of $1.6 billion due to the recent market turmoil and concerns that tariffs may have on Versace's business, according to a Financial Times report on Wednesday, which cited people familiar with the matter. An announcement is expected Thursday, though timing could slip, and the price tag could change.

Capri CEO John Idol is in Milan to complete the details with the Prada family, according to the report.

Prada and Capri both declined to comment to the FT.

The FT item also comes amid a Wall Street Journal report on Wednesday that a $1.4 billion Versace deal could be at risk due to recent market tumult and tariff worries. The report said that the deal had 50-50 odds of completion, according to a person familiar with the matter. 

Reuters reported on Sunday that Prada (OTCPK:PRDSF) will make a decision  on a possible takeover of rival Versace this week.

data.title: SA Analyst Rating Downgrades: VZ, BB, AAPL, PATH
data.description:
A roundup of the most recent downgrades issued by SA analysts.

Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL). Buy to sell by The Techie. "The new tariffs and economic uncertainty are expected to hamper smartphone demand and pressure Apple's margins, making the current stock price unattractive as more downside is coming. Diversification efforts in production are ongoing, but not enough to offset China's dependency."

Verizon (NYSE:VZ). Buy to hold by The Alpha Sieve. "VZ's valuations are not cheap, particularly in light of the earnings growth on offer this year, while its yield is roughly on par with other global telecommunication services stocks."

BlackBerry (NYSE:BB). Buy to hold by First Principles Partners. 

UiPath (NYSE:PATH). Buy to hold by May Investing Ideas.

data.title: China points to Boeing woes, U.S. infrastructure in strained trade relations
data.description:
In a newly released white paper on China-U.S. trade relations, Beijing has directly blamed multiple U.S. shortcomings — including limited production capacity, inadequate infrastructure and issues with key exporters like Boeing (NYSE:BA) — for impeding progress under bilateral trade agreements.

The document outlines China’s perspective on trade challenges over the past several years, arguing that despite its efforts to fulfill obligations under the China-U.S. Phase One trade agreement, it has faced numerous roadblocks originating from the American side.

“China’s performance of its obligations under the agreement has encountered multiple obstacles caused by the U.S.,” according to the report from the State Councial Information Office for the People's Republic of China. Among the highlighted issues: Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) aircraft delivery shortfalls during the pandemic, subpar quality of agricultural exports and logistical bottlenecks in U.S. ports.

The white paper comes as the United States and China escalate their trade war with significant jumps in tariffs on each other's goods. U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariffs on about 100 nations went into effect overnight and included a 104% tariff on Chinese imports. On Wednesday, Beijing said it would raise levies on U.S. imports to 84% from 34% previously.

Boeing’s production cuts, safety issues

A key point of contention is the performance of U.S. aerospace giant Boeing (BA), whose output of planes has been hampered by safety issues, regulatory scrutiny, supply-chain constraints and labor strife.

“In 2020, Boeing's aircraft production was only about 40 percent of its 2019 output, which significantly impacted deliveries to China,” the paper notes.

Additionally, the paper references the 737 Max crisis, citing the aircraft’s two fatal crashes in 2018 and 2019 and subsequent global grounding as a major disruption to aviation trade. 

“Most countries worldwide, including China and the U.S., grounded the aircraft model, dealing a significant blow to the aviation trade,” the report states.

Seeking Alpha asked Boeing (BA) to respond to China's report, and will update this article accordingly.

Agricultural exports hampered

China also attributes delays and declines in agricultural imports to conditions beyond its control. Poor U.S. weather conditions in 2019 were blamed for excessive levels of ergot and vomitoxin in wheat, which didn't meet China’s food safety standards in 2020.

Moreover, the white paper emphasizes U.S. agricultural price disadvantages and quality concerns as factors reducing Chinese buyer interest.

“U.S. soybeans are at a price disadvantage compared to South American soybeans; U.S. beef is significantly more expensive… U.S. rice can hardly compete with Southeast Asian rice in terms of quality, appearance, taste and price,” China asserts.

In one example, the report claims that in February 2020, U.S. rice was RMB3,000 to RMB3,500 more expensive per tonne than Thai and Vietnamese rice, respectively.

Infrastructure, logistics bottlenecks

The report devotes significant attention to U.S. infrastructure shortcomings, particularly in ports and energy transport.

“Most U.S. ports in the Gulf of Mexico cannot directly accommodate very large crude carriers… This results in U.S. crude oil transport costs to China tripling those from the Middle East,” the paper alleges.

The Covid-19 pandemic further strained logistics, with widespread congestion reported at major U.S. ports. Citing data from the Shanghai Shipping Exchange, the paper compares the average port stay duration in 2021 at Los Angeles and Long Beach — more than 10 days — to less than three days at Shanghai and Shenzhen ports.

“Ports and other infrastructure in the U.S. were already in a tight balance,” the paper says. “With the impact of Covid-19… various supply chain links… struggled to adapt.”

Beijing’s broader message

While the white paper stops short of absolving China of all responsibility for trade imbalances, it frames the U.S. as a primary source of disruption in fulfilling mutually agreed commitments. The overall tone reflects growing Chinese frustration with what it views as systemic and structural limitations on the American side of the trade relationship.
